segments.s590-g41: This is a nominal phrase with two coordinated subjects: 'چوپان و لشکر همه' (the shepherd and the entire host). 'فروماند' is a past-form predicate modifying that compound subject and here means 'fell into a state of exhaustion/dismay' (an intransitive past). Word order is Verb‑Subject inversion typical in poetry, where the predicate precedes the coordinated nouns.
segments.s590-g42: 'برآشفت' is a past verb meaning 'grew agitated/took fright' and continues the description of what happened; the prepositional/ablative 'ازان' ('from that') introduces the source or cause of the agitation, followed by 'شهریار رمه' ('that sovereign’s flock/ herd') as the noun phrase affected. The clause as a whole says the flock was alarmed by that ruler.
segments.s590-g43: 'هم‌انگاه' is an adverb meaning 'immediately.' 'برداشت' is a past transitive verb here meaning 'he took up/removed' (sense: lifting/removal), and it governs the direct objects 'زین و لگام' (saddle and bridle). The personal subject is omitted (understood from context as the king).
segments.s590-g44: This is a locative/result clause: 'به نزدیک آن اسپ' = 'the horse that was near (him)' and 'شد شادکام' = 'became joyful/fortunate.' 'به نزدیک' functions adverbially ('near'), 'آن اسپ' is the noun phrase, and 'شد' is the past copula/verb 'became' taking the predicate adjective 'شادکام.'
segments.s590-g45: 'چنان ... شد' is a correlative structure meaning 'so/thus ... became.' 'رام شد' = 'became tame/subdued' and 'خنگ' qualifies the subject as 'dazed/stupefied.' 'بر جای خویش' is a locative phrase 'in its own place' (خویش = its own; here the enclitic ش marks possession).
segments.s590-g46: The subordinating 'که' introduces the result clause: 'ننهاد دست' is a negative past-form verb phrase ('did not put/raise a hand'), where 'دست' functions as a periphrastic reference to limb/hoof. 'از پس و پای پیش' lists hind and fore ('from the hind [leg] and the front [leg]') — 'از' = 'from/of', while 'پس' here denotes the rear (not fully reflected in the provided sense list). The whole clause says it did not move a limb of either the hind or the front.
segments.s590-g47: 'ز شاه جهاندار' is an agent/ablative phrase 'by/from the king, the sovereign' (poetic 'ز' = 'by' or 'from'). 'بستد' is the past transitive 'bound/tied,' and its object is 'لگام' (the bridle). The phrase reports the king’s action of fastening the bridle.
segments.s590-g48: 'به زین بر نهادن' uses the infinitival/gerundial 'نهادن' (to put/place) with the preposition 'به' giving an instrumental/causal nuance ('by/when placing on the saddle'). 'همان گشت رام' = 'that same (horse) became tame' where 'گشت' is past 'became' and 'رام' the resulting adjective. The clause links the placing of the saddle to the horse’s becoming tame.
segments.s590-g1: بدو (به + او) is a prepositional phrase 'to him' placed before the verb, a literary order; گفت is used as the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided senses list lacks the verbal sense, so this is a spoken-verb usage); موبد is the speaker noun 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest). که‌ای شهریار is a vocative: که here introduces the direct speech and ای (rendered together as که‌ای) calls 'O' to the addressee, شهریار = 'king'.
segments.s590-g2: بگشتی is the perfect/preterite form (2nd person) meaning 'you turned/returned' or 'you have turned' — here used to ask whether one has departed from something; تو is the explicit subject 'you' (redundant for emphasis); از + راه = 'from the path' (از = from, راه = path); پروردگار is the object 'the Lord/God'. Word order is literary but straightforward: Verb (بگشتی) before subject (تو) and followed by the prepositional phrase.
segments.s590-g3: تو گفتی is 'you said' (گفتی is the 2nd-person past), که introduces reported content. بگریزم is the present-subjunctive form with first-person ending (-م) but here functions in reported speech as 'would escape/you would escape' (classical Persian often preserves subjunctive morphology in reported clauses); از چنگ مرگ = 'from the clench/claws of death' (از = from, چنگ = claw/clutch, مرگ = death).
segments.s590-g4: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'; باد خزان = 'the autumn wind' (باد = wind, خزان = autumn); آمد is the past 'came'. از شاخ برگ literally 'from bough to leaf' (از = from, شاخ = branch/bough, برگ = leaf), a poetic image explaining the inevitability of leaves falling — the clause is comparative/illustrative following the reported claim.
segments.s590-g5: ترا (ت + را written separately here) = 'to you' or 'your' (object/possessive sense), with را acting as the object/emphatic marker attached; چاره اینست = 'this is the remedy' (اینست = 'this is'); کز (contraction of که + از) introduces 'from' or 'that from' and here links to the following prepositional phrase; راه شهد = 'the way/path of sweetness' (شهد = honey/sweetness) — a metaphorical phrase meaning indulgent/pleasant ways.
segments.s590-g6: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'; چشمه = 'spring' (a source); سو here also carries the directional sense 'toward' (the two elements form a compound directional phrase in poetry: 'toward the spring, toward ...'); گرایی is a deverbal/nominal element meaning 'inclination/turning' (here 'turning toward'); به مهد = 'to the cradle' (به = to/toward, مهد = cradle/birthplace). The phrase instructs movement away from sweetness toward the originating spring/cradle (figurative).
segments.s590-g7: نیایش is the noun 'prayer/supplication'; کنی is the present-subjunctive/imperative-like form of کردن (to do) 2nd-person — here an exhortation 'you should perform/pray'; پیش یزدان پاک = 'before the pure God' (پیش = before/in the presence of, یزدان = God, پاک = pure). The verb is in the subjunctive used as a polite or hortative command.
segments.s590-g8: بگردی is the present-subjunctive of گشتن (to go about/roam/turn) used hortatively 'go about' or 'make yourself go' (here instructing the addressee); به زاری = 'in/with lamentation' (به = with/toward, زاری = lamentation); بران = 'upon that/thereon' (literary deictic referring back to the place/earth); گرم here means 'fervent/zealous'; خاک = 'earth/soil' (i.e. prostrate on the ground). The cluster forms a command: go about in fervent lamentation, prostrating on that earth.
segments.s590-g9: بگویی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive (or optative) form meaning 'you might say' or 'one might say'; که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; من بنده ناتوان is a humble self-reference in subject position with adjective after noun (بنده 'servant' + ناتوان 'powerless'), literally 'I, a powerless servant.'
segments.s590-g10: زده here is a participial/past adjective meaning 'struck' or 'afflicted' and combines with دام 'snare/trap' (زده دام = 'struck/ensnared by a trap'); سوگند names the trap ('the oath'); پیش is a prepositional particle here meaning 'in the presence of / upon', and روان is a poetic noun 'soul/spirit' — together indicating being ensnared by an oath upon the soul.
segments.s590-g11: کنون آمدم is a perfective past ('now I have come' / 'I came now'); تا here functions like 'now that' / 'so that' introducing the following question about fate/appointed time; زمانم is 'my time' (possessive suffix -م); کجاست is the fused interrogative 'where is it' (کجا + ست/copula).
segments.s590-g12: به پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before you' (به + پیش used idiomatically); این داور is the noun phrase 'this arbiter/judge' placed before its modifiers; داد و راست are two coordinated abstract nouns 'justice and right/truth' describing the judge — word order is typical classical Persian where predicate/noun phrases can follow the locative phrase.
segments.s590-g13: چو بشنید is a temporal subordinate 'when he heard'; شاه is the subject; آن پسند آمدش is an idiomatic expression 'that pleased him' (پسند 'approval/pleasure' + آمدش 'came to him' = 'it pleased him'). Note the verb آمدش is enclitic (verb + third-person object/benefactive).
segments.s590-g14: همان درد را places emphasis on 'that very sorrow/grievance' (همان = 'the same/that', درد = 'sorrow/affliction', را marks the definite object/topic); سودمند آمدش is another compound where سودمند 'useful/advantageous' + آمدش 'came to him' means 'seemed/useful to him' or 'appeared advantageous to him.'
segments.s590-g15: بیاورد is a past/telic verb 'he brought' or 'caused to bring'; سیصد عماری is a numeral + noun 'three hundred buildings/dwellings' (عماری here = dwellings/edifices); و مهد links another noun 'and a cradle/birthplace' — classical Persian often strings items with و without an extra copula.
segments.s590-g16: گذر کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'he passed' (گذر 'passage' + کرد 'did/made' = 'passed by'); بر سوی means 'toward' (بر + سوی = toward/to the direction of); دریای شهر is a genitival phrase 'the city's sea' — in epic/poetic language this can mean the harbor/sea beside the city or a figurative great expanse associated with the city.
segments.s590-g57: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when/if'. از گردش او combines از ('from') + گردش ('course, turning, sway') + او ('his') to mean 'from his turning/sway'; word order is preposition + noun + possessive pronoun. نیابی is the 2nd-person singular present negative/ability form of یابیدن ('to find/attain') here meaning 'you do not/cannot find'; رها is a noun/adjective 'release, freedom' (or 'free') and is the object/result you fail to find.
segments.s590-g58: پرستیدن is an infinitival/masdar form 'worshiping' functioning as the subject/topic; او is the object 'him'. نیارد here is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person verb meaning 'does not bring' / 'will not yield' (negative result), and بها is the direct object meaning 'value/worth' — together 'worshiping him will not bring value'.
segments.s590-g59: به یزدان گرای is an imperative/hortative instruction 'turn to God' where به + یزدان = 'to God' and گرای is a verbal/poetic form meaning 'turn (toward)'. و joins the clauses. بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto him', کن is the imperative 'do/make' (here 'take/make'), and پناه is the noun 'refuge' — together 'and to him make refuge' = 'take refuge in Him'.
segments.s590-g60: خداوند here is a title 'the Lord' (one who rules), followed by گردنده 'turner/one who makes revolve' modifying خورشید ماه ('sun [and] moon') — a compact epithet meaning 'the Lord who causes the sun and the moon to revolve'. Word order: head noun (خداوند) + participial modifier (گردنده) + the things governed (خورشید ماه).
segments.s590-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. او کشته شد is a passive/perfect construction 'he was killed' (کشته = 'killed/slain', شد as auxiliary). اسپ آبی is the noun 'steed/horse' followed by its adjective 'blue' (typical Persian adjective-after-noun order). چوگرد is a terse/poetic particle here meaning 'as/so' or functioning as a linking/exclamatory particle ('and so'); it is a poetic marker rather than a separate full clause verb.
segments.s590-g62: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بران is a deictic/prepositional adverb 'to/upon that' (بران = بر + آن, 'to that place/thing'). چشمه is 'spring' and لاژورد is the material/color 'lapis-lazuli/azure', together 'that lapis-lazuli spring' — 'came to that lapis spring.'
segments.s590-g63: به آب اندرون شد is literally 'into the water entered' — the preposition به + noun آب + اندرون ('inside') + شد (past auxiliary) yields 'entered into the water'. تنش is تن ('body') with the enclitic possessive ش = 'his' (his body). ناپدید is an adjective/past participle 'vanished/disappeared' describing the result.
segments.s590-g64: کس اندر جهان این شگفتی ندید reads 'no one in the world saw this wonder.' کس here functions negatively as 'no one' (classical Persian often uses کس ... ندید = 'no one saw'), اندر = 'in', جهان = 'world', این = 'this', شگفتی = 'marvel/wonder', and ندید is the past negative 'did not see'.
segments.s590-g25: منی کرد و گفت is a short reporting clause: منی کرد is an archaic/poetic expression meaning ‘made obeisance / humbled himself’ (منی here is not the modern pronoun but part of the fixed verb), کرد is the simple past ‘did’, and و گفت (‘and said’) introduces direct speech. The sequence Verb (کرد) + و + گفت is a common compound narrative formula in older Persian for ‘he did X and said…’.
segments.s590-g26: نشستن چه بایست چندین به جای is the content of the utterance. نشستن is the infinitive/noun ‘to sit / sitting’; چه here means ‘how’ (in interrogative/indirect-question sense); بایست is the modal ‘ought / must’ (poetic form of باید); چندین means ‘several’ or ‘many a’; and به جای literally ‘to/for a place’ — together: ‘how one ought to sit, how many should occupy a place’. Word order places the verbal-nominal phrase (نشستن) first, then the interrogative (چه), then the modal (بایست), and finally the quantifier + location phrase.
segments.s590-g27: چو گردنکشی کرد شاه رمه is a temporal clause: چو = ‘when’; گردنکشی is a noun meaning ‘defiance / stubbornness’; کرد is the past-tense ‘did / showed’; and شاه رمه (‘king of the flock’) is a compound title = leader of the herd. The clause therefore reads ‘when the flock’s king showed defiance…’.
segments.s590-g28: که از خویشتن دید نیکی همه is a subordinate/casual clause introduced by که (here ‘for / because’). از + خویشتن = ‘from himself / in himself’; دید is the past ‘he saw / perceived’; نیکی همه = ‘all goodness’. Together: ‘for he perceived all goodness in himself.’
segments.s590-g29: ز دریا برآمد یکی اسپ خنگ is a simple narrative clause: ز (poetic for از) = ‘from / out of’; دریا = ‘the sea’; برآمد = ‘emerged / came forth’ (past); یکی = ‘a certain / one’; اسپ = ‘steed / horse’; خنگ = an adjective meaning ‘dull / stupid’. The adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian narration.
segments.s590-g30: سرین گرد چون گور و کوتاه لنگ describes the animal’s body: سرین = ‘rump / hind‑quarters’ (here the posterior of the horse), گرد = ‘round’ (adjectival), چون = ‘like’, گور = ‘gūr’ (here the wild ass / onager as a point of comparison), و کوتاه لنگ = ‘and short and lame’. Adjectives follow the noun and are coordinated by و.
segments.s590-g31: دوان و چو شیر ژیان پر ز خشم is another predicative group: دوان (poetic past/preterite form) = ‘it ran / rushed’; و = ‘and’; چو شیر ژیان = ‘like a living lion’ (ژیان ‘life / living’ used adjectivally to mean ‘alive’); پر ز خشم = ‘full of anger’. The simile (چو شیر ژیان) modifies the manner of running and the horse’s demeanor.
segments.s590-g32: بلند و سیه‌خایه و زاغ‌چشم is a string of adjectives describing the creature: بلند = ‘tall / lofty’; سیه‌خایه = ‘dark‑flanked / sooty‑sided’; زاغ‌چشم = ‘black‑eyed / dark‑eyed’. The adjectives are simply coordinated by و and follow the noun they describe, as is typical in Persian.
segments.s590-g33: کشان is a present-participle/adverbial form of کشیدن, here functioning as 'trailing/dragging'. دم در پای is a locative idiom 'tail at (their) feet' where در = 'at/in' and پای = 'foot(s)'; the phrase describes how the tail hangs low. با + یال و بش links the following body-parts/features with 'with'; یال = 'mane' is straightforward, while بش in this context is a noun whose exact sense is unclear in the provided senses (see mapping).
segments.s590-g34: This is a string of coordinated descriptive nouns/adjectives: سیه سم is a compound describing 'black-hoofed' (سیه modifies سم), کفک‌افگن is a participial/compound adjective 'foam-throwing' (i.e. foaming), and شیرکش is an agent-noun 'lion-slayer' used as an epithet. The coordinating و simply links these attributive terms.
segments.s590-g35: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'); با + مهتران یزدگرد = 'with the chiefs of Yazdegerd' where با marks accompaniment and یزدگرد is the king's name. Note: the supplied senses for گفت and مهتران did not include the ordinary past-verb and plural 'chiefs', so senses are marked accordingly.
segments.s590-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'/'so that'); این را is the direct object marked by را ('this' as definite object); سپاه is the noun 'army' and اندر = 'in/into' gives the locative/goal; آرید is an archaic/poetic form of آوردن ('to bring') used here in the sense 'bring/let be brought'; گرد functions adverbially as 'around/in here' (or 'about'). The clause as a whole is an indirect order: 'that this army be brought in/around.'
segments.s590-g37: بشد is the past form of شدن 'to become' used here to mark an action/state change ('turned' / 'it happened'); گرد = 'around/about' completes the verbal phrase (بشد گرد = 'turned about' / 'gathered'); چوپان و ده کره‌تاز is the coordinated subject 'the shepherd and ten foals' (کره‌تاز = 'young foal').
segments.s590-g38: یکی = 'one (of them)' or 'a certain one'; زین 'saddle' and پیچان کمند دراز form a coordinated nominal phrase 'and a twisting/coil­ing lasso long' (پیچان = 'winding/twisting' modifies کمند = 'lasso', with دراز = 'long' placed after the noun as a postpositive adjective). Word order shows typical Persian modifier placement: modifiers may precede (پیچان) or follow (دراز) the noun.
segments.s590-g39: چه دانست = 'what/how did (he) know' (چه = interrogative 'what/how', دانست = past 'knew'); راز is the direct object 'secret'; جهاندار شاه is a titular phrase 'world-holder (i.e. sovereign) king' where جهاندار modifies شاه.
segments.s590-g40: که introduces the content of the secret ('that ...'); آوردی here is a past/perfect form of آوردن 'you brought' or 'you have brought' (used to report the action); این اژدها را is the definite object 'this dragon' (این + را), and به راه = 'onto/to the road' (به = 'to/into/towards', راه = 'road/path'). The clause reports what was brought and is subordinate to دانست.
segments.s590-g17: شب و روز is a temporal fronted expression 'night and day'. بودی is an archaic/past copula meaning 'was' (here used for the narrative third-person), followed by the locative phrase به مهد اندرون 'into/within the cradle' (به + noun + اندرون gives a direction/location). Word order is typical poetic Persian: time phrase → copula → locative phrase.
segments.s590-g18: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بینیش here denotes the source (literally 'his بینی' = his nose/sight) and is followed by the adverb گه‌گه 'occasionally'. همی is the classical habitual/continuous particle that marks recurring action in narrative, and رفت is the past verb 'went/flowed' placed before its noun خون (poetic inversion). The whole clause means 'blood would occasionally flow from his nose'.
segments.s590-g19: چو is the conjunction 'when'. نزدیکی 'nearness' governs the following locative phrase چشمه سو ('toward the spring', with سو = 'toward/side'), and رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived'. The phrase is a temporal subordinate: 'When (he) came near the spring...'.
segments.s590-g20: برون آمد is a compound past 'came out/forth'. از مهد 'from the cradle' is the source phrase, و links the next clause, and دریا بدید uses a poetic inversion (object دریا before the verb بدید 'saw') meaning 'and (he) saw the sea'. All verbs are past narrative forms.
segments.s590-g21: ازان 'from/that' + آب 'water' sets the referent. لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while'. به سر بر نهاد is a classical verbal phrase: بر نهاد (past of نهادن, 'placed') with the prepositional phrase به سر 'to/onto the head' — here idiomatically 'dipped/put his head (into the water)'. The verb نهاد is the past tense 'placed'.
segments.s590-g22: ز یزدان 'to/from God' marks the addressee/agent of the petition. نیکی دهش is a noun phrase 'grant/boon of goodness' (دهش = 'gift/grant'); کرد is the past verb 'made/did' and یاد here functions as 'remembrance/appeal' — classical phrasing meaning 'he made a remembrance to God, asking for goodness' (i.e., he prayed for a good grant). Word order places the noun phrase before the verb in typical poetic style.
segments.s590-g23: زمانی here means 'for a time/for a while'. نیامد is the negative past 'did not come/occur'. ز بینیش خون is the prepositional source phrase 'blood from his nose' (again بینیش = 'his nose/sight'); the clause reads 'for a while blood did not come from his nose'. The negative verb precedes the source phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s590-g24: بخورد here is used in the sense 'to befall/happen' (not 'to eat'), referring back to the blood event; و simply links clauses. بیاسود is a past-form of 'آسودن' meaning 'rested/was at ease' in narrative (classical usage). با رهنمون 'with the guide' is a prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment or under guidance. Together: '...and he rested with the guide.'
segments.s590-g73: This is a prepositional phrase giving the container/location: به + تابوت زرین = 'into/into a golden coffin' (به here marks direction/placement). The coordinator و links a second locative phrase در مهد ساج = 'in a cradle of saj (wood)', where در is the locative preposition and مهد ساج is a genitive phrase 'cradle of saj.' Word order places the goal/container before the verb that follows.
segments.s590-g74: سوی پارس شد is a directional clause 'was moved/took [the direction] toward Pars' with the finite verb شد; the grammatical subject آن خداوند تاج ('that lord/sovereign — the crown') comes after the verb (postposed for poetic emphasis). Here شد expresses the resulting motion/state ('was carried/was made to go').
segments.s590-g75: چنین است introduces a statement 'thus it is,' followed by the predicate noun phrase رسم سرای بلند = 'the custom of the lofty house/palace.' سرای here is a genitive head meaning 'house/palace' (note: the provided lemma senses do not list that meaning, see senses). The order places the general comment ('چنین است') before the specific noun phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s590-g76: This is a conditional/temporal admonition: چو آرام یابی = 'when/if you find rest' — یابی is a present/subjunctive form ('you come to/find'), and بترس is an imperative 'fear!/beware.' از گزند uses از to mark the source/what to fear: 'fear (from) harm.'
segments.s590-g77: تو رامی is a simple clause 'you are rami/tame' (رامی here used adjectivally). The second clause با تو جهان رام نیست places the prepositional phrase با تو 'with you' before the subject جهان and predicate رام نیست 'is not tamed' — a contrastive construction: though you are tame, the world does not become tame with you.
segments.s590-g78: چو نام خورده آید is a temporal clause 'when renown/name comes' — خورده is a past participle attached to نام in an idiomatic sense (see note) and آید is the verb 'comes.' The following verbless comparative phrase به از جام نیست = 'there is nothing better than the cup' is elliptical: به از + X + نیست expresses 'nothing is better than X.'
segments.s590-g79: پرستیدن دین is a verbal-noun phrase 'worshipping the religion/faith' serving as the subject. بهست از introduces a comparison 'is preferable/better than' (A بهست از B = 'A is better than B'), so the whole phrase means 'worshipping the faith is better than sin.'
segments.s590-g80: چو باشد کسی را بدین پایگاه is a conditional clause 'if/when someone is in this station/position.' بدین is a fused prepositional item (ب + this) meaning 'in/with this'; را here marks the person in an older/dative-like usage ('for someone' / 'someone'), and پایگاه = 'position/base/station.'
segments.s590-g65: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; it introduces the source 'لشکر' (the host/army). The noun phrase 'لشکر خروشی' ('an army-roar') is the theme, and the past verb برآمد ('arose/issued forth') follows it. چو introduces a simile 'like' and takes the simile noun کوس ('war-drum') after it. Word order is source → noun phrase → verb → simile marker → simile noun.
segments.s590-g66: که here introduces the content of the cry (a reported clause). شاها is a vocative 'O king' addressing the addressee. زمان is the subject 'time' and آوریدت is an older/poetic 3rd‑person form with the enclitic object -ت 'you' (meaning 'brings you' / 'has brought you'); the preposition به marks the destination 'طوس' (Tus). Note the archaic verb morphology and the vocative inserted at the clause start.
segments.s590-g67: همه جامه‌ها را places the definite object 'all the garments' before the verb; را is the object marker. The compound verb phrase بکردند چاک literally 'they made (چاک)' = 'they tore (them)'. The verb is past plural 'they did/made' and takes the noun 'چاک' as its result/complement.
segments.s590-g68: همی is a continuity/iterative particle ('were/about to / kept on'); ریختند is the past plural of ریختن 'to pour/throw/dispense' and here describes the mourners' action. The prepositional sequence از بر + [یال و خاک] is idiomatic: from/on/over the head/hair (یال) and the dust/ground (خاک) — i.e. they cast dust upon their hair and the earth. The verb comes before the prepositional phrase; the subject is implied 'they'.
segments.s590-g69: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that'. The verb form بکافید is archaic/poetic; in this construction it precedes the noun موبد (the priest), which functions as the agent and is postposed for emphasis. برش is the direct object 'a cut / incision'. The clause therefore means roughly 'after that the mobed (did) a cut' — note the nonstandard/poetic ordering and an older verbal form.
segments.s590-g70: میان marks the locative relation 'between'. تهیگاه 'the hollow/empty place' and مغز 'brain' are the two terms joined by و. سرش is 'his head' with the enclitic possessive -ش; word order places the possessed noun (سر) immediately before the enclitic. The whole phrase is a prepositional complement to the preceding verb: 'between the hollow cavity and the brain of his head.'
segments.s590-g71: بیاگند is an archaic/poetic verb here relating to treating the corpse so it will not corrupt (the lexical senses offered are 'to rot / to corrupt' but the context shows the verb governs the treatment). یکسر is an adverb 'wholly / entirely' modifying the verb. به + کافور و مشک indicates the substances used 'with camphor and musk' (instrumental phrase); و simply connects the two aromatics.
segments.s590-g72: به دیبا marks the material or covering 'in/with brocade' (به + noun). تنش را is the definite object 'his body' with the possessive -ش and object-marker را placed before the verb. بکردند is the past plural 'they made/did' and خشک is a resultative adjective 'dry' placed after the verb; together the sequence means 'they wrapped/made his body dry (in brocade).' The verb-object order and postposed adjective are standard in such resultative constructions.
segments.s590-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. زین بر نهادش is a verbal phrase: نهاد is the past stem 'put/placed' with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it'), and بر + زین marks 'put the saddle on (him)'. برآهخت is a past action (third-person) 'raised/reared' or 'drew up/tightened' depending on context and takes تنگ (here adverbial 'tightly/closely') as its modifier; word order places the object and preposition (زین بر) before the verb and its pronominal object suffix.
segments.s590-g50: نجنبید is a past negative verb 'did not move/stir' (third-person). بر جای is a locative phrase 'on/at the place', where بر takes جای ('place') as its object. تازان نهنگ is a noun phrase in which تازان (used adjectivally here) qualifies نهنگ: 'the fierce/mighty nِehang' (a metaphorical great steed); Persian often stacks modifiers after the head.
segments.s590-g51: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/thereupon'. پای او شد is a small clause 'his foot became/shifted' (شد as copula/auxiliary). که introduces a result or explanatory clause ('so that/so as to'), and بنددش is here a verbal notion with the clitic -ش (object 'it/him') related to the following noun دم; the phrase indicates that the foot’s motion resulted in something happening to the tail/moment (دم) — syntactically, Persian often strings such short result clauses after شد.
segments.s590-g52: خروشان شد is a compound where خروشان (roaring) is predicated with شد 'became/was', giving 'it roared' or 'became roaring'. آن باره is a temporal adverbial 'at that moment/that occasion' (آن = that/then, باره = occasion), and سنگ سم is an adjectival comparison 'stone-hoof' or simile 'the hoof like a stone' (سنگ modifying سم), describing the blow’s hardness.
segments.s590-g53: بغرید is a past verb 'it bellowed/roared'. و is the simple conjunction 'and'. یک جفته زد is an idiomatic expression 'struck a single (paired) blow' (جفته 'pair' used to indicate a double stroke) with زد 'struck' as the main verb; بر برش places the blow 'upon/against him' (بر + برش).
segments.s590-g54: به خاک اندر آمد is a locative motion phrase 'fell to / came into the dust/earth' with به indicating direction ('into') and اندر آمد 'came/arrived into'. سر و افسرش lists two objects joined by و: سر 'head' and افسرش 'his helmet' (افسر with pronominal -ش): both are the things that 'came into the dust'.
segments.s590-g55: ز خاک آمد is a simple past-voice motion 'came from the earth/dust' with the poetic preposition ز = 'from'. و خاک شد Yezdgerd uses شد 'became' to state the result 'became dust'; یزدگرد is the proper name (subject) attached to the second clause. The sequence compresses motion and result in two short clauses.
segments.s590-g56: This is a direct question: چه جویی تو = 'what are you seeking, you...' where چه is the interrogative 'what' and جویی is the verb/noun 'seek/search' (here functioning as the predicate). زین بر شده is a participial phrase 'having mounted / become on the saddle' (زین = saddle, بر = on, شده = become/been), and هفت‌گرد is vocative or name (here treated as a proper name/place-name), addressing the addressee.

sentences.s590-s21: The shepherd and the whole host were dismayed, and the flock took fright at that sovereign.
sentences.s590-s22: At once he lifted the saddle and the bridle; the horse near him became joyful.
sentences.s590-s23: So subdued and dazed it became in its place that it did not lift a hoof, neither hind nor fore.
sentences.s590-s24: The sovereign bound the bridle; when he set the saddle on it, that same (horse) became tame.
sentences.s590-s1: The mobed said to him, "O king, have you turned away from the path of the Lord?"
sentences.s590-s2: You said you would flee the grasp of death; when the autumn wind came, the leaves from the bough (fell).
sentences.s590-s3: Your remedy is this: depart from the path of sweetness and turn toward the spring, toward the cradle.
sentences.s590-s4: Pray before the pure God; go about in fervent lamentation, prostrate upon that earth.
sentences.s590-s5: You might say that I, a helpless servant, have been ensnared by the snare of an oath upon (my) soul.
sentences.s590-s6: Now I have come; where is my time? Before you stands this arbiter of justice and right.
sentences.s590-s7: When the king heard it, he was pleased; that very sorrow seemed advantageous to him.
sentences.s590-s8: He brought three hundred dwellings and a cradle, and then passed toward the city's sea.
sentences.s590-s29: If you cannot find release from his sway, worshiping him will bring no worth.
sentences.s590-s30: Turn to God and take refuge in Him — the Lord who sets the sun and moon in motion.
sentences.s590-s31: When he was slain, a blue steed came to that lapis-lazuli spring.
sentences.s590-s32: His body entered the water and vanished; no one in the world had seen such a wonder.
sentences.s590-s13: He made obeisance and said, “This is the custom and counsel — how one should sit, and how many should take a place.”
sentences.s590-s14: When the leader of the flock showed defiance — for he perceived all goodness in himself.
sentences.s590-s15: Out of the sea came a certain dull (or stupid) steed; its rump round like an onager’s, and it was short and lame.
sentences.s590-s16: It ran, and like a living lion (it) was full of anger — tall, dark‑flanked and black‑eyed.
sentences.s590-s17: Trailing their tails at their feet, with manes and (besh), black-hoofed, foam-flinging, and lion-slaying.
sentences.s590-s18: Thus Yazdegerd spoke with his chiefs, saying that this army should be brought in.
sentences.s590-s19: The shepherd and ten young foals turned about; one (of them) had a saddle and a long, coiling lariat.
sentences.s590-s20: How could the sovereign king know the secret that you brought this dragon onto the road?
sentences.s590-s9: Night and day he was inside the cradle; from his nose blood would occasionally flow.
sentences.s590-s10: When he drew near the spring he reached it; he came out of the cradle and saw the sea.
sentences.s590-s11: He briefly put his head into that water; he prayed to God, asking for goodness.
sentences.s590-s12: For a time no blood came from his nose, and he rested with the guide.
sentences.s590-s37: That sovereign of the crown was carried toward Pars in a golden coffin and in a cradle of saj-wood.
sentences.s590-s38: Such is the custom of the lofty house; when you find repose, fear harm.
sentences.s590-s39: You are tame, and yet with you the world is not tamed; when renown comes, there is nothing better than the cup.
sentences.s590-s40: Worshipping the faith is better than sin, if anyone is in this position.
sentences.s590-s33: From the host there rose a cry like a war-drum, as if saying, "O King, time brings you to Tus."
sentences.s590-s34: They tore all their garments into rags; they threw dust upon their hair and the earth.
sentences.s590-s35: After that, the mobed made a cut between the hollow cavity and the brain of his head.
sentences.s590-s36: They anointed him all over with camphor and musk; they wrapped his body in brocade and made it dry.
sentences.s590-s25: When he set the saddle on him and drew it tight, the mighty charger did not stir from its place.
sentences.s590-s26: Then his foot moved so that his tail was caught; with a roar, at that instant the hoof struck like a stone.
sentences.s590-s27: It bellowed, and with a double blow struck him; his head and helmet fell into the dust.
sentences.s590-s28: Yazdegerd came up from the dust and turned into dust; 'What are you seeking, you who have mounted this saddle, Haftgerd?'
